title: Advanced Compound Adj.
theory:
Theory

Compound adjectives are made of two or more words joined by a hyphen. They function as a single idea to describe a noun.

1. The Golden Rule: Number-Noun
Numbers + Nouns are ALWAYS SINGULAR when used as adjectives.

"A five-star hotel." (Correct)

"A five-stars hotel." (Incorrect)

2. Common Combinations
Pattern Example
Adverb/Adj + Past Participle "Well-known actor", "Old-fashioned clothes".
Noun/Adj + Present Participle "Good-looking guy", "Time-saving device".
Noun + Adjective "World-famous singer", "Ice-cold water".
Number + Noun "Ten-minute walk", "Twenty-year-old girl".
3. Real-Life Scenarios
Describing People
  • "He is a broad-minded person." (Open to ideas)
  • "She is extremely hard-working."
  • "My boss is short-tempered." (Gets angry easily)
  • "They are a well-behaved class."
  • "He is a left-handed player."
Products & Places
  • "We bought a brand-new car."
  • "It's a state-of-the-art facility." (Modern)
  • "I need a hands-free device."
  • "This is a sugar-free drink."
  • "It was a last-minute decision."
practice: {"games": [{"type": "multiple_choice", "title": "Sophisticated Adjectives", "instruction": "Choose the most appropriate advanced compound adjective.", "questions": [{"q": "A ______ performance (very impressive).", "options": ["breath-taking", "heart-breaking", "mind-blowing"], "a": "breath-taking"}, {"q": "A ______ child (always talking).", "options": ["quick-witted", "smooth-tongued", "sharp-tongued"], "a": "quick-witted"}, {"q": "A ______ problem (long-lasting).", "options": ["long-standing", "far-reaching", "deep-seated"], "a": "long-standing"}, {"q": "A ______ decision (important for future).", "options": ["far-reaching", "level-headed", "open-minded"], "a": "far-reaching"}, {"q": "An ______ student (thinking about others).", "options": ["open-minded", "absent-minded", "strong-willed"], "a": "open-minded"}]}, {"type": "fill_in", "title": "Advanced Compound Fill C1", "instruction": "Type the compound adjective (use hyphens).", "questions": [{"q": "A view that takes your breath away is ______.", "a": "breath-taking"}, {"q": "A person who is not prejudiced is ______.", "a": "open-minded"}, {"q": "A result that is very good is ______.", "a": "top-notch"}, {"q": "A person who forgets things easily is ______.", "a": "absent-minded"}, {"q": "A task that requires a lot of time is ______.", "a": "time-consuming"}]}]}

title: Relative Pronouns
description: Know when relative pronouns are mandatory or optional
theory:
Theory

Relative pronouns (who, which, that, whom, whose) connect clauses. Knowing when you can delete them distinguishes a native speaker from a learner.

1. When can you OMIT the pronoun?

YES: When the pronoun is the OBJECT of the verb.

  • "The man (who/that) I met." → "The man I met."
  • "The book (which/that) she bought." → "The book she bought."

NO: When the pronoun is the SUBJECT of the verb.

  • "The man who called me." (Cannot say: "The man called me" - changes meaning)
  • "The machine that broke."
2. Whom & Whose
Pronoun Usage Example
Who Subject (Person) "The girl who won."
Whom Object (Person) - Formal "The girl whom I saw." (Often omitted or replaced by 'who' in speech)
Whose Possession "The boy whose bike was stolen."
3. Real-Life Scenarios
Daily Speech (Omitting)
  • "Here's the money you lent me." (Object)
  • "Is that the movie we watched?" (Object)
  • "The food she cooked was delicious." (Object)
  • "The guy I told you about is here." (Object + Preposition)
  • "I have everything I need."
Formal/Required
  • "To whom it may concern." (Formal Object)
  • "The employee whose performance improved." (Possession)
  • "We need staff who can speak French." (Subject - Required)
  • "The problem that caused the delay." (Subject - Required)
  • "He's the one who did it." (Subject)
practice: {"games": [{"type": "multiple_choice", "title": "To whom, for which", "instruction": "Choose the correct preposition + relative pronoun.", "questions": [{"q": "The man ______ I spoke is here.", "options": ["to whom", "who", "which"], "a": "to whom"}, {"q": "The city ______ I live is big.", "options": ["in which", "where", "both are correct"], "a": "both are correct"}, {"q": "The reason ______ he left is unknown.", "options": ["for which", "why", "both are correct"], "a": "both are correct"}, {"q": "The tool ______ he worked was sharp.", "options": ["with which", "which", "that"], "a": "with which"}, {"q": "The company ______ she works is tiny.", "options": ["for which", "whom", "where"], "a": "for which"}]}, {"type": "fill_in", "title": "Prepositional Relative Fill C1", "instruction": "Type: to whom, in which, for which, etc.", "questions": [{"q": "The person ______ I was talking is my boss.", "a": "to whom"}, {"q": "The building ______ they live is old.", "a": "in which"}, {"q": "The goal ______ we aim is high.", "a": "at which"}, {"q": "The friend ______ I went out is Spanish.", "a": "with whom"}, {"q": "The book ______ I was reading is gone.", "a": "which"}]}]}

title: As if / As though / Like
description: Make comparisons and hypotheses with precision
theory:
Theory

These phrases describe how something seems or is done. The key C1 nuance is knowing when to use the unreal past.

1. As if / As though

They mean the same thing (Similarity). The verb tense tells you if it's real or not.

Situation Tense Used Example
Real / Possible Normal Tense (Present) "He looks as if he is sick." (He probably is).
Unreal / Hypothetical Past Tense (Unreal) "He acts as if he were the boss." (He is NOT the boss).
2. Like vs As vs The way
  • Like: Followed by a Noun/Pronoun (Informal conjunction).
  • "He runs like a leopard." (Simile)
  • As: Followed by a Clause (Subject + Verb) or Preposition phrase.
  • "Do as I say." / "He works as a doctor." (Role)
  • The way: Informal equivalent to "as" or "how".
  • "I hate the way he looks at me."
3. Real-Life Scenarios
Pretending & Impressions
  • "She talks as if she knew everything." (She doesn't)
  • "It smells as though someone has been smoking." (Real possibility)
  • "He spends money like water." (Idiom)
  • "Why do you look at me like that?"
  • "He commands them as if he were a general." (Unreal)
Instructions & Roles
  • "Leave everything as it is."
  • "He works as a consultant." (His real job)
  • "Do it the way I showed you."
  • "It looks like it's going to rain." (Prediction)
  • "Treat me like a friend."
practice: {"games": [{"type": "multiple_choice", "title": "It seems like...", "instruction": "Choose the correct hypothetical structure.", "questions": [{"q": "He talks as ______ he were the boss.", "options": ["if", "though", "both are correct"], "a": "both are correct"}, {"q": "It looks ______ it's going to rain.", "options": ["like", "as if", "both are correct"], "a": "both are correct"}, {"q": "She acted as if she ______ me.", "options": ["didn't know", "doesn't know", "hasn't known"], "a": "didn't know"}, {"q": "You look as though you ______ a ghost.", "options": ["had seen", "saw", "see"], "a": "had seen"}, {"q": "They feel like they ______ on holiday.", "options": ["are", "were", "be"], "a": "were"}]}, {"type": "fill_in", "title": "Hypothetical Fill C1", "instruction": "Type: as if, as though, or the correct verb form.", "questions": [{"q": "He behaves ______ he owned the place.", "a": "as if"}, {"q": "You look ______ you haven't slept.", "a": "as though"}, {"q": "It feels ______ summer is over.", "a": "like"}, {"q": "She treated me as if I ______ (be) a child.", "a": "were"}, {"q": "You sound as though you ______ (catch) a cold.", "a": "had caught"}]}]}

title: Subjunctive Verbs
description: Use the subjunctive for formal recommendations and wishes
theory:
Theory

The Subjunctive is used for demands, suggestions, and necessity. The key rule: use the BASE FORM of the verb (no 's', no 'ed', no 'ing').

1. The Formula
Trigger + 'That' + Subject + Base Verb

"I suggest that he go home." (Not 'goes')

"It is vital that she be present." (Not 'is')

2. Common Triggers
Category Verbs / Adjectives
Verbs Advise, Ask, Demand, Insist, Propose, Recommend, Request, Suggest.
Adjectives Essential, Imperative, Important, Necessary, Vital.
Negative "I insist that he not be late." (Do not use 'don't' or 'doesn't')
3. Real-Life Scenarios
Professional Requests
  • "We request that the meeting start on time."
  • "The boss demands that he submit the report."
  • "I propose that we take a break."
  • "It is crucial that the system remain secure."
  • "They asked that I be interviewed."
Advice & Urgency
  • "The doctor recommends that she rest."
  • "It is essential that you not eat before surgery."
  • "I insist that you stay for dinner."
  • "It is necessary that he see a specialist."
  • "My advice is that he find a new job."
practice: {"games": [{"type": "multiple_choice", "title": "It is essential that...", "instruction": "Choose the correct subjunctive form.", "questions": [{"q": "It is important that he ______ here.", "options": ["be", "is", "was"], "a": "be"}, {"q": "I suggest that she ______ harder.", "options": ["study", "studies", "studied"], "a": "study"}, {"q": "It is essential that we ______ on time.", "options": ["arrive", "arrives", "arrived"], "a": "arrive"}, {"q": "He demanded that the door ______ unlocked.", "options": ["be", "is", "was"], "a": "be"}, {"q": "I recommend that he ______ a doctor.", "options": ["see", "sees", "seeing"], "a": "see"}]}, {"type": "fill_in", "title": "Subjunctive Fill C1", "instruction": "Type the base form of the verb (e.g., be, study, go).", "questions": [{"q": "It is mandatory that every student ______ (have) an ID.", "a": "have"}, {"q": "I propose that the meeting ______ (be) postponed.", "a": "be"}, {"q": "The doctor suggested that he ______ (rest).", "a": "rest"}, {"q": "It is crucial that she ______ (not / tell) him.", "a": "not tell"}, {"q": "They requested that we ______ (be) present.", "a": "be"}]}]}

title: Subject-Verb Agreement
description: Match verbs correctly with quantifier subjects
theory:
Theory

Matching the verb to the subject can be tricky with quantifiers. Is it singular or plural? It often depends on the noun that follows.

1. The "Number" Rule
A number of...

Meaning: "Many"

PLURAL

"A number of cars are waiting."

The number of...

Meaning: "The specific figure"

SINGULAR

"The number of cars is small."

2. Of-Phrases & Portions

For All of, Most of, Some of, A lot of, None of, Permissions (Percentages/Fractions), look at the noun after 'of'.

Noun Type Verb Form Example
Singular / Uncountable Singular "All of the cake was eaten."
Plural Plural "All of the cookies were eaten."
Each / Every Singular (Always) "Each of the students has a book."
3. Real-Life Scenarios
Data & Statistics
  • "A number of issues have been reported."
  • "The number of issues has decreased."
  • "50% of the budget is gone."
  • "50% of the employees are on strike."
  • "None of the information is accurate."
Groups & Individuals
  • "Each of us is responsible."
  • "Neither of them likes it." (Formal: usage implies singular)
  • "The majority of people agree." (Collective plural)
  • "Everybody knows the truth."
  • "Some of the team are away." (Meaning members)
practice: {"games": [{"type": "multiple_choice", "title": "Singular or Plural?", "instruction": "Choose the correct verb based on advanced agreement rules.", "questions": [{"q": "Neither the teacher nor the students ______ here.", "options": ["are", "is", "was"], "a": "are"}, {"q": "Each of the children ______ a gift.", "options": ["receives", "receive", "receiving"], "a": "receives"}, {"q": "The news ______ shocking.", "options": ["was", "were", "are"], "a": "was"}, {"q": "Politics ______ a complex subject.", "options": ["is", "are", "be"], "a": "is"}, {"q": "Ten dollars ______ too much.", "options": ["is", "are", "were"], "a": "is"}]}, {"type": "fill_in", "title": "Agreement Fill C1", "instruction": "Type the correct verb form (e.g., is, are, has, have).", "questions": [{"q": "Statistics ______ (be) my favorite subject.", "a": "is"}, {"q": "The group ______ (have) different opinions.", "a": "has"}, {"q": "Either the cats or the dog ______ (be) in the house.", "a": "is"}, {"q": "A number of people ______ (be) waiting.", "a": "are"}, {"q": "The number of students ______ (be) increasing.", "a": "is"}]}]}
